{{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site | WHS = Kumano Sanzan - Nachi no Ōtaki | image = Kumano Kodo World heritage Nachi-no-taki 熊野古道 那智大滝10.JPG | image_upright = 1.2 | caption = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, [[Kii Peninsula]], {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, [[Honshu]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | part_of = {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|qid=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} | criteria = {{UNESCO WHS type|(ii), (iii), (iv), (vi)}}(ii), (iii), (iv), (vi) | ID = 1142bis-011 | coordinates = {{coord|33.672|N|135.891|E|display=inline, title|source:cswiki|format=dms}} | year = 2004 | extension = 2016 | area = {{convert|2.5|ha|acre|abbr=on}} | locmapin = Japan | map_caption = }} {{nihongo|'''Nachi Falls'''|那智滝|Nachi no Taki}} in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, is one of the best-known {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|qid=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s in Japan. With a drop of 133 meters (and 13 meters wide),<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau|last = |first = }}</ref> it is the tallest waterfall with a single uninterrupted drop in Japan (although Japan also disputes Russia's right to {{ill|Iturup|en|Iturup|qid=Q215727|lt=Iturup|1=Iturup}} Island, which has the 141 m high {{ill|Ilya Muromets Waterfall|en|Ilya Muromets Waterfall|qid=Q649996|lt=Ilya Muromets Waterfall|1=Ilya Muromets Waterfall}}); however, the tallest Japanese waterfalls with multiple drops are {{ill|Hannoki Falls|en|Hannoki Falls|qid=Q2967719|lt=Hannoki Falls|1=Hannoki Falls}}, at 497 m (seasonal), and {{ill|Shōmyō Falls|en|Shōmyō Falls|qid=Q731954|lt=Shōmyō Falls|1=Shōmyō Falls}}, at 350 m (year round).
There are two rocks at the top of the falls that are the guardian kami of the falls and the Shinto shrine. There was also a Buddhist temple here that was destroyed during the Meiji Restoration (late 19th century). Many [[Shugendō|''shugenja'']] and star-crossed lovers have leaped from the top of the waterfall in the belief that they will be reborn into Kannon's paradise. Early each morning, the Shinto priest makes offerings to the waterfall in a ritual.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Places.htm|title = Places of Interest at Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref> In 1918, a {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} mound was excavated at the base of the waterfall and found to contain many important archaeological artifacts, including statues, mirrors, altar fittings and Sutra cylinders. These are now displayed in the Ryuhoden (“Treasure Hall”), located next to the Sanjūdō Pagoda (the 3-story pagoda). These Sutra mounds were created by priests in times of war to hide their treasures but also many items were buried in this way as a result of the belief that the end of the world was coming at the start of the 10th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/History.htm|title = History of Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
Believed to house a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]] worshiped at [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is part of the "{{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|qid=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}" [[:en:UNESCO World Heritage Site|UNESCO World Heritage Site]].
<gallery> File:Nachi Falls.jpg|upright=0.8|Hanging scroll of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (13th–14th century) depicting the falls|alt=Very tall and narrow depiction of a waterfall. There are large trees at the bottom of the fall and small trees at the top of the rock face. The sun (or moon) is half visible behind the rock.
